data.title: MarketEnterprise to Record 69 Million Yen Gain From Stock Forward Cancellation
data.description:
MarketEnterprise will book a 69 million yen gain from the cancellation of a stock forward transaction in non-operating income for the nine months ended March 31, according to a Wednesday filing on the Tokyo Stock Exchange.

The gain was not factored into the full-year forecast for the fiscal year ending June 30, disclosed in August 2024.

data.title: Evolution Mining Says Western Australia Gold Mine Mill Begins Commissioning After Expansion
data.description:
Evolution Mining said the commissioning of the mill at the Mungari gold mine in Western Australia began after the process plant capacity was expanded to 4.2 million tonnes per annum from 2 million tonnes per annum, according to a Thursday Australian bourse filing.

The project team at the mill handed over to the operations team. Commissioning is set to continue through the June quarter, the filing said.

The current estimated final cost of the expansion is AU$228 million.

The expansion will allow the mine life of the Mungari mine to be extended to at least 2038, per the filing.

data.title: Trump's Reciprocal Tariffs May Hurt Apple's Gross MarginMarket Talk
data.description: President Trump's reciprocal tariffs could hurt Apple's gross margin, three analysts at Citi Research say in a research report. Assuming that Apple can't get exempted this time and that Apple gets hit by the U.S.'s accumulative 54% tariffs on China and doesn't pass this through, there could be around an estimated 9% negative effect on the company's total gross margin, the analysts say. Citi estimates Apple presently has more than 90% of its manufacturing in China. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

data.title: Confusion, Frustration at Produce Industry Meeting
data.description:
By Eliza Collins

TUBAC, Ariz.-Dozens of produce-industry executives were gathered in the ballroom of an Arizona golf resort watching as President Trump ticked down a list of countries getting hit with new tariffs-holding their breath for him to get to Mexico. When Trump concluded remarks without a clear answer on how much the U.S. would be taxing Mexican imports, the program at the Fresh Produce Association of the Americas spring summit resumed but the anxiety didn't go away.

Panelists would pause their remarks to read off text messages and news headlines speculating on possible tariffs on Mexico. Many attendees at the meeting were either Mexican exporters or affiliated with the U.S. import market. According to the FPAA, Mexico represents roughly four of 10 imported fruits and vegetables consumed throughout North America. The international group, gathered here half an hour from the U.S.-Mexico border, will be dramatically affected by new tariffs, attendees said.

"I think [Trump administration officials] underestimate the negative impacts this causes," said John Pandol, the director of special projects for Pandol, a company that grows and also imports grapes and blueberries from Mexico, Chile and Peru. Pandol said prices were likely to go up with new tariffs. "You can't put an extra 10 or 20 or 30% in a cost and it just get absorbed," he said. "No, there's not that kind of fat in the system."

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1912 ET - President Trump's tariff "blow (is) bigger than we expected," Capital Economics' Neil Shearing and Paul Ashworth say in a research report. Capital Economics' calculations imply an import-weighted average tariff of 19.1%, the economists say. If reciprocal tariffs stack on top of earlier tariff increases on China and product-specific tariffs on steel, aluminum and autos, this means effective tariff rate on all imports will rise to around 26% from 2.3% last year, the economists estimate. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

1901 ET - U.S. President Trump's tariff announcement has "caused some impressive gyrations in markets," IG's Chris Beauchamp says in an email. "The full range of tariffs deals a remarkable blow to the global trading system, as the U.S. enacts steep charges on its main trading partners," the chief market analyst say. "This scenario is far worse than had been anticipated. After a brief relief rally (overnight), the outlook for equity markets seems grim," Beauchamp says. This news has sent equity futures sharply lower, Beauchamp adds. eMini NASDAQ 100 futures last down 4.3%; eMini S&P 500 futures last 3.5% lower; eMini Dow futures last 2.4% down. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

1855 ET [Dow Jones]--RH could have been more conservative with its guidance if it would have known what Trump's reciprocal tariffs plan was going to be like, CEO Gary Friedman says on a call with analysts. "We gave guidance that was appropriate," Friedman says. "Maybe we would have been even more conservative had we known." The furniture company will now take time to analyze the impact of reciprocal tariffs on its operations with a long-term focus. "Welcome to a new world," Friedman says. Shares fall 25% to $185.98 in postmarket trading. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com)

1857 ET - Wayfair and other furniture companies shifted sourcing to a variety of Asian countries after President Trump's first term posed steep levies on China, the company's Chief Executive Niraj Shah said on an earnings call in February. "There's sources inside Asia, so places like Cambodia, Indonesia, Thailand, Philippines and Vietnam for sure, that have grown as places where folks have factories and where our goods are coming from," Shah told analysts at the time. The stock's fall, down 15% in after-hours trading, suggests its efforts to shift its supply chain could now be in vain as Trump's new tariffs promise some of the biggest levies on several of its now-key suppliers, including Indonesia and Vietnam, with levies of 32% and 46%, respectively.(kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1856 ET - U.S. equity futures have fallen more than 2.3% following President Trump's far more hawkish than expected tariff announcements. The tariff rates far exceed baseline expectations, and if they aren't negotiated down promptly, expectations for a recession in the U.S. will rise dramatically, says Tony Sycamore, market analyst at IG. Markets are very likely to see Goldman Sachs revise higher their 12-month estimate of a recession in the U.S. from 35% to 50%, he adds. U.S. equity markets aren't priced for a recession, and in the event of a recession in the U.S., stock markets could easily fall another 10%-15% below their mid-March lows, he adds. (james.glynn@wsj.com; @JamesGlynnWSJ)

1842 ET - President Trump's tariff announcement is "worse than the worst case scenario" the street was fearing, say Wedbush analysts Daniel Ives and Scott Devitt. While many details are to be worked out, investors will focus on specifics in the next 24 hours. The jaw-dropper was the 34% reciprocal tariff on China, followed by 32% on Taiwan, along with EU at 20%, they say in a note. Tech stocks will be under pressure due to concerns about demand destruction, supply chain disruptions, and particularly the China-Taiwan aspect of the tariffs, they say. (venkat.pr@wsj.com)

1839 ET - Australian stock futures are pointing to an opening rise for the S&P/ASX 200 index, although movements in other markets point to the possibility of early volatility. ASX futures are up by 0.5% ahead of Thursday's session, which is scheduled to begin about two hours after President Trump unveiled a 10% blanket tariff on Australian imports. The benchmark share index in New Zealand, which is subject to the same levy, dropped as much as 1.7% in early trade before paring its deficit to about 0.6%. Ahead of the open, Treasury Wine Estates said it expects only a minimal impact from tariffs. Fisher & Paykel Healthcare, which is listed in both Australia and New Zealand, said it anticipates additional costs from fiscal 2026. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

1838 ET - RH has been further diversifying its manufacturing network since the company realized China was going to be a long term problem, CEO Gary Friedman says on a call with analysts. "Now it looks like moving out of China didn't make a difference," the executive says. The game has changed and it doesn't matter where you go, except for America and the U.S., where the company has been expanding its capabilities, he says. "It's going to be messy, but it's going to be messy for everyone," Friedman says, while acknowledging that he doesn't like to be caught off guard. Overall, RH can navigate the current environment "pretty well" over the short term, the executive adds. RH shares fall 25% to $186 in post-market trading. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1831 ET - VF, which owns brands including North Face, Vans and Timberland, has been waging a turnaround. President Trump's sweeping new tariff plan that calls for the U.S. to impose a 10% tariff on all imports and much higher rates for some nations, will likely make its revitalization efforts more difficult. During its fiscal 2024, VF's products were sourced from independent manufacturers primarily located in Asia, the company said in its most recent annual filing with the Securities and Exchange Commission. A slew of events, including 34% duties on China, could hurt the company's ability to source or deliver products, as well as its profitability, it says. Shares fall 5.2% in late trading. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1809 ET - Australia's leading business lobby group is urging the country's government not to retaliate in kind against U.S. tariffs. The Business Council of Australia says it is deeply disappointed by the size of the tariffs, which it believes will hurt economic growth. Even so, the industry group backs Australia's current policy of non-retaliation. "There are no winners in a trade war, which would only risk making our situation worse by forcing Australians to pay more and reducing job security," BCA Chief Executive Bran Black says. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

1743 ET - New Zealand's NZX-50 tumbled in early trade as shares in medical-device maker Fisher & Paykel Healthcare slumped on the announcement of U.S. tariffs. The benchmark index was down by 1.6% after about 40 minutes of Thursday's session, with Fisher & Paykel by far the biggest drag. Shares in Fisher & Paykel, the country's second-largest company by market capitalization, which sources about 45% of its production from Mexico, slumped 7.1%. Reacting to President Trump's announcement, Fisher & Paykel said it faced additional costs. Retirement-home operators, real-estate developers, and consumer-related stocks were among the other losers. The NZX-50 has lost about 7.6% so far in 2025. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

1735 ET - Lululemon Athletica thought it was safe from President Trump's tariffs. That wasn't the case. The Vancouver-based company doesn't make its yoga pants and running shorts in Canada, which previously gave it a competitive advantage over other Canadian companies that do manufacture domestically, such as Canada Goose. The company, however, produces nearly 90% of its athletic gear in Vietnam, Cambodia, Sri Lanka, Indonesia and Bangladesh. Shares fall 12% in late trading after President Trump announced reciprocal tariffs of 46% on Vietnam, 49% on Cambodia, 44% on Sri Lanka, 32% on Indonesia and 37% on Bangladesh. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

data.title: Economists: Tariffs to Raise $700 Billion For U.S. and Boost Inflation
data.description:
By Ryan Dezember

Capital Economics estimated that the import taxes outlined by President Trump will generate roughly $700 billion a year in customs duties for the U.S. government.

The tariffs will raise a maximum of $835 billion, the firm's economists calculate. But "assuming such high tariffs lead to a marked decline in imports, the increase in revenues will probably end up closer to $700 billion," they wrote in a note to clients. That's equivalent to 2.3% of the country's gross domestic product, they wrote.

Meanwhile, the economists say that since imports account for about 10% of consumption, the roughly 25% effective tariff rate that they calculate will add about 2.5% to consumer prices, lifting inflation to above 4% by the end of the year.

"Under those circumstances, we continue to expect the Fed to hold interest rates unchanged this year," they wrote.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: ASTI Holdings Seeks Extension to Hold 2024 AGM by August
data.description:
ASTI Holdings has applied for an extension to hold its annual general meeting for the financial year 2024 by Aug. 15, citing audit delays following the completion of prior years' financial statements, according to a Wednesday filing on the Singapore Exchange.

The company said its auditors, Forvis Mazars, began work on the FY24 audit only after their reappointment in February and need additional time, especially due to the liquidation of subsidiary Dragon Group International.

ASTI also plans to seek approval from Singapore's corporate regulator for a corresponding extension.

data.title: Treasury Wine Sees Minimal Impact From US Tariffs
data.description:
Treasury Wine Estates said it does not expect the US government's 10% tariff on imports from Australia and New Zealand to have a material impact on its business, according to a Thursday filing with the Australian bourse.

The company's Treasury Americas division generates about 15% of its revenue from Australian and New Zealand wines, with the rest coming from US-produced wines, the filing said.

data.title: US businesses brace for more pain as Trump rolls out reciprocal tariffs
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump announced a slew of reciprocal tariffs on trading partners on Wednesday, the latest in a series of extensive duties imposed since his return to the White House earlier this year.

The U.S. will now impose a 10% baseline tariff on all imports to the country effective April 5 and higher duties on several of its biggest trading partners.

The levies could be particularly damaging for U.S. companies in sectors already slapped with duties.

The following U.S. business sectors are most exposed to Trump's reciprocal tariffs:

AUTOS & ELECTRIC VEHICLES

Trump announced 25% tariffs on auto imports last week on all cars and parts made outside the United States. The duties will go into effect on Wednesday.

The Detroit Three automakers - Ford , General Motors and Stellantis - have a much bigger exposure to manufacturing outside of the U.S. vis-à-vis Elon Musk's Tesla , and face a bigger hit.

CHIPS

Semiconductors were not named in the list of goods subject to reciprocal tariffs, despite Trump stating in February that he intended to impose tariffs of around 25% on the imports of semiconductors and related products.

The 34% tariffs on China, however, could prove disruptive for chips, PCs, semiconductors and server manufacturers, industry experts said.

U.S. chip companies and artificial intelligence networking equipment makers have complex and diverse supply chains spanning multiple countries, with executives saying the near-term fallout from any tariffs will be "uncertain."

Industry heavyweight Nvidia's CEO has said the company plans to move manufacturing domestically in response to higher tariffs but expects little short-term impact.

PHARMACEUTICALS

Pharmaceutical products, long spared from trade wars due to the potential harm, were exempt from Wednesday's announcement, a temporary relief for drugmakers.

U.S. drugmakers have been lobbying Trump to phase in tariffs on imported pharmaceutical products in hopes of reducing the sting from the charges and to allow time to shift manufacturing.

Trump is planning other tariffs targeting pharmaceuticals, an official said.

PC MAKERS

PC makers such as Dell face the risk of potential price increases driven by the tariffs on China.

Research firm International Data Corporation lowered its traditional PC forecast for 2025 and beyond in February due to tariff-related risks.

RETAIL & E-COMMERCE

Walmart , Target , Best Buy and several other U.S. retailers have been bracing for a likely hit from the tariffs, especially on key supplier China. The companies have been piling pressure on suppliers over prices on categories ranging from toys to cake pans.

Trump imposed 46% tariff on Vietnam, which will likely impact apparel and sportswear makers from Nike to On Holding , as they source about half of their products from the country.

Alcohol makers — including Brown-Forman , Diageo , Anheuser-Busch , Constellation Brands , Molson Coors Beverage — have also been caught in the cross-fire, following the tariffs on goods from Canada, Mexico and China imposed in February.

Mexico and Canada avoided fresh tariffs on Wednesday with Trump exempting the United States' top trading partners from his new 10% global tariff baseline, although previous duties remain in place.

Separately, the removal of the trade loophole called "de minimis", which allows goods valued at less than $800 to enter the U.S. duty-free and with minimal inspections, will hit discount-price online retailers as well.

E-commerce players such as Shein, PDD Holdings-owned Temu and Alibaba's AliExpress all rely on the de minimis exemption to keep prices low.

ENERGY & RESOURCES

Import of oil, gas and refined products was exempted from Trump's sweeping new tariffs, the White House confirmed on Wednesday.

But, separate tariffs on critical minerals were under consideration. Uranium prices for U.S. companies could rise by 10% if tariffs are implemented, said Canadian uranium miner and producer Cameco , weighing heavily on the country that relies primarily on imports of the ore.

BANKS

The industry was expected to benefit from the Republican Party's sweeping election victory in November, with investment banks anticipating a rise in dealmaking and capital markets activity.

However, economic uncertainty - partly driven by tariffs - has dampened that optimism.

The KBW Regional Banking Index has fallen nearly 7%. Profit estimates are at risk as uncertainty curbs sentiment and prompts businesses to adopt a wait-and-see approach, analysts at Raymond James warned in a note on Tuesday.

TRAVEL

Tourists and companies have reduced spending amid the rising economic uncertainty, forcing carriers to cut their first-quarter expectations. Some airlines have also started culling flights to avoid lowering fares and to protect margins.

data.title: New Zealand Stocks Drop After Trump's Tariffs
data.description:
The stock market in New Zealand fell 113 points, or 0.9%, to 12,208 in morning trade on Thursday after being flat the day before, amid worries of a potential global trade war following Trump's tariffs announcement.

The NZX tracked a plunge in U.S. stock futures, as the market reacted to Trump's tariffs announcement.

Trump would impose a 10% baseline tariff on all imports to the U.S. and higher duties on some of the country's biggest trading partners, including responding to what he said was a 20% tariff on U.S. goods from New Zealand.

The baseline tariff is 10%, which will apply to New Zealand.

Almost all sectors traded in the red, with energy falling the most, followed by industrials, materials, and utilities.

Among the biggest laggards were Skellerup Holdings (-1.7%), F&C Investment Trust (-1.6%), ANZ Group (-1.2%), AFT Pharmaceuticals (-1.2%), Gentrack Group (-1.2%), Fisher & Paykel (-0.9%), and A2 Milk (-0.8%).

data.title: Raffles Education Issues SG$1.8 Million in Seventh Tranche Bonds to CEO
data.description:
Raffles Education has issued SG$1.8 million in unlisted, non-convertible bonds to its chairman and chief executive officer, Chew Hua Seng, under the seventh tranche of its bond program, according to a Wednesday filing with the Singapore Exchange.

The proceeds will be used to refinance the group's borrowings. Despite efforts to secure external investors, the company allocated the tranche to Chew, who also subscribed to the first tranche.

data.title: US TARIFFS STATE OF PLAY: Pres Trump Imposes Massive Global Tariffs Likely to Cause Widespread USA Price Hikes
data.description:
WASHINGTON (MaceNews) – Amid much pessimism about severe economic domestic consequences, President Trump Wednesday imposed massive tariffs on several dozen countries including China and the European Union and a blanket 10% levy on all the rest.

The Rose Garden announcement shook stock futures into large negatives while interest rates maintained lows last hit in October. The U.S. 10-year yield was at 4.13%.

One CNBC commentator said the new tariffs were the “worst of the worst case scenarios,” with an additional 34% levy on China goods on top of the already imposed 20%. The EU nations get 20%, Japan 24%. The already-announced 25% tariff on all imported cars and parts goes into effect at midnight while the latest so-called reciprocal tariffs hit April 5 and April 9.

A minimum 10% baseline reciprocal tariff will be imposed on all imports from anywhere if not covered by the higher tariff rates announced Wednesday such as is the case for the UK and Scotland. Otherwise, in what President Trump said was a “kind” gesture, the reciprocal tariffs are being set at only half the rates that would truly be symmetrically recriprocal.

For Canada and Mexico, no change, as explained in a White House fact sheet: This means USMCA compliant goods will continue to see a 0% tariff, non-USMCA compliant goods will see a 25% tariff, and non-USMCA compliant energy and potash will see a 10% tariff. In the event the existing fentanyl/migration IEEPA orders are terminated, USMCA compliant goods would continue to receive preferential treatment, while non-USMCA compliant goods would be subject to a 12% reciprocal tariff.

Exempt from any of the reciprocal tariffs are those categories to be subject to “sectoral” tariffs, such as copper, pharmaceuticals, semiconductors, lumber, energy and “other certain minerals that are not available in the United States.”

Any retaliation answering the day’s reciprocal tariffs will be answered with increased USA tariffs. But if other countries take significant steps to lower their tariffs then the USA tariffs can be decreased.

Other than China, Japan and EU tariffs, those countries described as “worst offenders” get hit with levy rates up to 50%. For example, India gets a 26% rate, Vietnam faces a 50% tariff rate, Cambodia 49 %.

The jarring realignment of global trade arrangements – the most severe in many, many decades – is on a tight schedule that will force customs agents, importers, suppliers and logistics specialists to scramble in figuring out what to do next. President Trump, who has yet to concede that American importing firms, not foreign governments actually pay the tariffs, spoke mostly in sweeping generalities about how the United States has suffered from trade abuse despite the negotiated and otherwise long-accepted trade arrangements built through the decades.

“We’re going to be an entirely different country,” Trump said. “It’s going to be fantastic for the workers.” Trump’s sweeping vision for the future means, he said, “There will never have been a transformation that’s already happening in the United States of America.. It’s an incredible thing to watch.”

But critics say that even if disruptive new tariffs eventually pull in new jobs for Americans, that transformation will take years to accomplish while meanwhile prices of an overwhelmingly large portion of purchased goods will go up. For all automobiles, those entirely manufactured across the border or those produced domestically but with tariffed engines, transmissions and many other components, the increased costs could be several thousand dollars per vehicle.

For President Trump, the changes have made him the center of attention around much of the world. As the tariffs prompt bilateral negotiations in the months ahead, the focus will remain on the White House while the next shoes – retaliatory tariffs, drop. Now come the price adjustments for hundreds of categories of foods and manufactured products as well as the supply constraints that will erupt for many popular imports, from alcoholic beverages to shoes, the “de-tox” period as described by Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent who says the storied American Dream no longer means cheap items from countries like China and Bangladesh.

data.title: Coca-Cola Bottlers Japan Buys Back 925,200 Shares
data.description:
Coca-Cola Bottlers Japan Holdings repurchased 925,200 shares for 2.24 billion yen between March 1 and March 31, according to a Wednesday filing on the Tokyo Stock Exchange.

The beverage company has now bought back 5.19 million shares under its current mandate, representing 2.86% of the 20 million-share authorization. The buyback program, approved in November 2024, allows repurchases of up to 30 billion yen through October.

data.title: Levi & Korsinsky Announces the Filing of a Securities Class Action on Behalf of TFI International Inc.(TFII) Shareholders
data.description:
NEW YORK, NY / ACCESS Newswire / April 2, 2025 / If you suffered a loss on your TFI International Inc. investment and want to learn about a potential recovery under the federal securities laws, follow the link below for more information:

https://zlk.com/pslra-1/tfi-international-inc-lawsuit-submission-form?prid=140601&wire=1

or contact Joseph E. Levi, Esq. via email at jlevi@levikorsinsky.com or call (212) 363-7500 to speak to our team of experienced shareholder advocates.

THE LAWSUIT: A class action securities lawsuit was filed against TFI International Inc. that seeks to recover losses of shareholders who were adversely affected by alleged securities fraud between April 26, 2024 and February 19, 2025.

CASE DETAILS: The filed complaint alleges that defendants made false statements and/or concealed that: (1) the Company was losing small and medium business customers; (2) as a result, the Company's TForce revenue was declining; (3) TFI was experiencing difficulties managing its costs; (4) as a result of the foregoing, the profitability of its largest business segment was declining; and (5) as a result of the foregoing, defendants' positive statements about the Company's business, operations, and prospects were materially misleading and/or lacked a reasonable basis.

WHAT'S NEXT? If you suffered a loss in TFI International Inc. stock during the relevant time frame - even if you still hold your shares - go to https://zlk.com/pslra-1/tfi-international-inc-lawsuit-submission-form?prid=140601&wire=1 to learn about your rights to seek a recovery. There is no cost or obligation to participate.

WHY LEVI & KORSINSKY: Over the past 20 years, Levi & Korsinsky LLP has established itself as a nationally-recognized securities litigation firm that has secured hundreds of millions of dollars for aggrieved shareholders and built a track record of winning high-stakes cases. The firm has extensive expertise representing investors in complex securities litigation and a team of over 70 employees to serve our clients. For seven years in a row, Levi & Korsinsky has ranked in ISS Securities Class Action Services' Top 50 Report as one of the top securities litigation firms in the United States. Attorney Advertising. Prior results do not guarantee similar outcomes.

data.title: Mesoblast Files Request for Type B Meeting with FDA to Discuss Accelerated Approval Pathway for Revascor® in Ischemic Heart Failure
data.description:
NEW YORK, April 02, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — Mesoblast (Nasdaq:MESO; ASX:MSB), global leader in allogeneic cellular medicines for inflammatory diseases, today provided an update on its plans to meet with the United States Food and Drug Administration (FDA) to discuss the accelerated approval pathway for Revascor® (rexlemestrocel-L) in the treatment of patients with ischemic chronic heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF) and inflammation.

Mesoblast filed a request for a Type B meeting during the last quarter, in line with previous guidance, and expects to have the meeting during this quarter. The meeting will be held under Mesoblast’s Regenerative Medicines Advanced Therapy (RMAT) designation for REVASCOR.

In a Type B meeting last year, FDA provided guidance to Mesoblast that the company was eligible to file for accelerated approval of REVASCOR in patients with end-stage HFrEF based on the totality of data across two randomized controlled trials. FDA also guided that a single confirmatory trial in class II/III patients with ischemic HFrEF and inflammation will need to be completed after any accelerated approval is obtained.

The key objectives of the meeting are to obtain FDA feedback on relevant chemistry, manufacturing & controls (CMC), alignment on potency assays for commercial product release, and Mesoblast’s proposed design and primary endpoint for the confirmatory trial.

About Mesoblast

Mesoblast (the Company) is a world leader in developing allogeneic (off-the-shelf) cellular medicines for the treatment of severe and life-threatening inflammatory conditions. The therapies from the Company’s proprietary mesenchymal lineage cell therapy technology platform respond to severe inflammation by releasing anti-inflammatory factors that counter and modulate multiple effector arms of the immune system, resulting in significant reduction of the damaging inflammatory process.

Mesoblast’s RYONCIL® (remestemcel-L) for the treatment of steroid-refractory acute graft versus host disease (SR-aGvHD) in pediatric patients 2 months and older is the first FDA approved mesenchymal stromal cell (MSC) therapy. Please see the full Prescribing Information at www.ryoncil.com.

Mesoblast is committed to developing additional cell therapies for distinct indications based on its remestemcel-L and rexlemestrocel-L allogeneic stromal cell technology platforms. RYONCIL is being developed for additional inflammatory diseases including SR-aGvHD in adults and biologic-resistant inflammatory bowel disease. Rexlemestrocel-L is being developed for heart failure and chronic low back pain. The Company has established commercial partnerships in Japan, Europe and China.

About Mesoblast intellectual property: Mesoblast has a strong and extensive global intellectual property portfolio, with over 1,000 granted patents or patent applications covering mesenchymal stromal cell compositions of matter, methods of manufacturing and indications. These granted patents and patent applications are expected to provide commercial protection extending through to at least 2041 in major markets.

About Mesoblast manufacturing: The Company’s proprietary manufacturing processes yield industrial-scale, cryopreserved, off-the-shelf, cellular medicines. These cell therapies, with defined pharmaceutical release criteria, are planned to be readily available to patients worldwide.

Mesoblast has locations in Australia, the United States and Singapore and is listed on the Australian Securities Exchange (MSB) and on the Nasdaq (MESO). For more information, please see www.mesoblast.com, LinkedIn: Mesoblast Limited and X: @Mesoblast

Forward-Looking Statements

This press release includes forward-looking statements that relate to future events or our future financial performance and involve known and unknown risks, uncertainties and other factors that may cause our actual results, levels of activity, performance or achievements to differ materially from any future results, levels of activity, performance or achievements expressed or implied by these forward-looking statements. We make such forward-looking statements pursuant to the safe harbor provisions of the Private Securities Litigation Reform Act of 1995 and other federal securities laws. Forward-looking statements should not be read as a guarantee of future performance or results, and actual results may differ from the results anticipated in these forward-looking statements, and the differences may be material and adverse. Forward-looking statements include, but are not limited to, statements about: the initiation, timing, progress and results of Mesoblast’s preclinical and clinical studies, and Mesoblast’s research and development programs; Mesoblast’s ability to advance product candidates into, enroll and successfully complete, clinical studies, including multi-national clinical trials; Mesoblast’s ability to advance its manufacturing capabilities; the timing or likelihood of regulatory filings and approvals, manufacturing activities and product marketing activities, if any; the commercialization of Mesoblast’s RYONCIL for pediatric SR-aGvHD and any other product candidates, if approved; regulatory or public perceptions and market acceptance surrounding the use of stem-cell based therapies; the potential for Mesoblast’s product candidates, if any are approved, to be withdrawn from the market due to patient adverse events or deaths; the potential benefits of strategic collaboration agreements and Mesoblast’s ability to enter into and maintain established strategic collaborations; Mesoblast’s ability to establish and maintain intellectual property on its product candidates and Mesoblast’s ability to successfully defend these in cases of alleged infringement; the scope of protection Mesoblast is able to establish and maintain for intellectual property rights covering its product candidates and technology; estimates of Mesoblast’s expenses, future revenues, capital requirements and its needs for additional financing; Mesoblast’s financial performance; developments relating to Mesoblast’s competitors and industry; and the pricing and reimbursement of Mesoblast’s product candidates, if approved. data.title: SEN. CORY BOOKER (D-NJ) IS INTERVIEWED ON MSNBC
data.description:
TRANSCRIPT

April 02, 2025

NEWS PROGRAM

SEN. CORY BOOKER (D-NJ)

SEN. CORY BOOKER (D-NJ) IS INTERVIEWED ON MSNBC

VIQ Media Transcription, Inc.

20 East Thomas Road, Suite 2200

Phoenix, AZ 85012

asc.info@viqsolutions.com

Copyright 2025. Provided under license from VIQ Media Transcription, Inc.

All materials herein are protected by United States copyright law

and/or license from VIQ Media Transcription, Inc., and may not be

reproduced, distributed, transmitted, displayed, published or

broadcast without the prior written permission of

VIQ Media Transcription, Inc.

You may not alter or remove any trademark, copyright or other

notice from copies of the content.

SEN. CORY BOOKER (D-NJ) IS INTERVIEWED ON MSNBC

APRIL 2, 2025

SPEAKERS:

SEN. CORY BOOKER (D-NJ)

ARI MELBER, HOST: I would say the man of the hour, but he might be the man of 25 hours.

Senator Cory Booker, thanks for joining me on "The Beat" tonight.

SEN. CORY BOOKER (D), NEW JERSEY: No, thank you. And there's an extraordinary man that mans this camera here for the — for your network, and he gave me a seat. So I want to bless him for that.

MELBER: You deserve a seat probably. Senator, we've set aside a good amount of time here because so many people want to hear from you. I have to be honest with you. We have not set aside 25 hours.

BOOKER: Ha! I'm grateful for that. That's a blessing too.

MELBER: We've heard you and we've heard why you did this. Would you be willing to share what you've heard back from your constituents, from citizens? Because now you've had some time to digest the response.

BOOKER: Not as much. My staff has sort of been dosing out some things to me as I've tried to, you know, just physically recover, even though my spirit is really soaring.

MELBER: Sure.

BOOKER: The gratitude from people whose voices I've elevated, who — who write to their senator with really personal, painful stories, to know that those people were in some ways affected by the fact that I read their letters and told their stories and talked to their pain and anger, that relieves me. I just want to make sure that I'm always respecting people who tell me about their challenges with Parkinson's or dementia, or their challenges with disabled children.

But to hear from Americans that feel really ignited and inspired, especially those who aren't that political or not that kind of base partisanship, that was really what I was dreaming of, is that somehow I could strike a chord that wasn't the normal left-right debates, but that in many ways could lift us beyond those to talk about a larger moral moment.

MELBER: We showed some of the international reaction, which, again, is not how the Senate, let alone the Democrats in the minority, have been heard from over the last months. You — you changed that. Here's some more of the political reaction we've seen.

(BEGIN VIDEO CLIP)

FRANK LUNTZ, POLLSTER & POLITICAL ANALYST: What Cory Booker did over the last 24 hours may have changed the course of political history. But he struck the kind of tone that grassroots Democrats are looking for.

CHARLAMAGNE THA GOD, CONTRIBUTOR, 'THE DAILY SHOW': A Democrat actually doing something, wow! I mean, wow, that's the longest someone's held Congress captive since January 6th.

SUNNY HOSTIN, CO-HOST, 'THE VIEW': We need that kind of energy coming from the Democrats. People feel that no one is fighting for them. And when I look at Cory Booker, I feel like Cory Booker is fighting for them.

(APPLAUSE)

(END VIDEO CLIP)

MELBER: We have some sense of what moved you to do this substantively, because we got to hear you explain it. I am curious, why now? Why this moment? You did it going into those elections, not knowing the outcome. You did it after some of these other court rulings. Enlighten us as to why now.

BOOKER: You know, we had a terrible fight in the Senate. And — and I love my 46 Democratic colleagues. But it was — if you were in those caucus meetings when we were debating over what to do with the continuing resolution, everybody who stood up actually had principled, strong feelings. But we were divided against each other on what to do. And I think I felt so, I won't use the word demoralized, but pretty down after that. And then I got it from people around my state who were just fit to be tied.

And it really — that was a time that it really came together for me, that I — enough is enough. I've been doing a role in my caucus right now of I believe in the people that I serve with, so I've been trying to get them to lean into social media where more and more of our voters are. And I've been proud of them. But I said, I — I need to step out and do something. And so I — I was hoping I could do something that would unify our caucus and unify people who believe that the country is facing really disastrous waters right now. And so I figured this was it.

And I'll have to say, I — I didn't know if I could last five hours...

MELBER: Sure.

BOOKER: ... 10 hours, 15 hours. But I always knew that out there, way out there, hoping it was within my reach, was the fact that a fierce segregationist was a guy that in this body, this noble body, hasn't always been right, has often been wrong, but over our history, I think the arc of our nation has bent more towards justice. But I thought it was an injustice that that record was held by that segregationist.

MELBER: You mentioned the segregationist Strom Thurmond. That has been part of the Senate history. If you read the Robert Caro books and the way the Senate rules were sometimes twisted to further the hand of segregation, and then, interestingly, sometimes the Senate rules allowed for progress, which is why we got, as you know, and I think viewers know, Civil Rights Act of '64 and the Voting Rights Act of '65. He was fighting against that progress. And so I briefly want to play a little bit about him, because you did break his record yesterday. Take a look.

(BEGIN VIDEO CLIP)

UNINDENTIFIED: James Strom Thurmond of South Carolina was known for one thing race, his opposition to integration in America.

SEN. STROM THURMOND (D), SOUTH CAROLINA: And I tell you, the American people from one side or the other had had better wake up and oppose such a program!

UNINDENTIFIED: He holds the all time filibuster record in the Senate. He once talked for 24 hours straight. The subject was civil rights.

(END VIDEO CLIP)

MELBER: He was on the wrong side of history, do you think some, not all, perhaps, but some of what Donald Trump is doing and those who acquiesce, who are quiet, or those billionaires who once criticized him and now join him, as a legal resident father is shipped away, are they on the wrong side of history?

BOOKER: Well, you know, you're making me emotional looking at that because while he was in the Senate, a place that Black people were never imagined to be, a brief period in Reconstruction in the 1800s, my family, my elders, were suffering at the hands of that kind of leadership. And, you know, my parents raised my brother and I in a very privileged, amazing town in New Jersey. But segregationists wouldn't let us move in. My parents had to get a white couple in 1969 to pose as my parents to put a bid on the house that was eventually accepted, and the closing came about.

And, you know, my dad and mom were very clear to my brother and I growing up in this relative privilege for two Black boys, to let us know whose shoulders we stand on, who bled, who sweat, who struggled so that we could have the opportunities. And my parents, if anything they commanded my brother and I to do, was to prove worthy of all those struggles and sacrifices.

So my mom, who was watching from her TV set, who did a sit-in in Charlotte, North — in Charlotte, Virginia, excuse me, who told me about the indignities and the suffering of growing up in a world that men like him so viciously segregated, there was a deep satisfaction in that, that — that thread that — that I hold on to, that is still being held by those ancestors who fought against that.

And so for me, that compelled me. I tell you, when — when members of the Congressional Black Caucus, who are my elders, some of them, some of them are much younger than me, but they stretch the gamut, when they started pouring into that chamber as I got closer and closer to that segregationist record, I just felt really lifted by them. I looked into the galleries and saw so many younger people than me, and then knew that we could change history together, not just in one guy's speech, but just like all those people who brought about the Civil Rights Act, who brought about the Voting Rights Act, that brought about the Fair Housing Act of '68 that really was the law that we had on our side to buy the home we bought. That we maybe could prove worthy of those blessings collectively.

And so when you bring up Donald Trump and some of the history of his family, I — I don't want to demonize him, because John Lewis and other marchers who stood up against the worst of the worst, they never, never gave in to hate. They never gave in to diminishing their character in order to aggrandize their own. But they talked about the issues. And I hope America right now doesn't get too caught up in making Donald Trump the center of their focus, because that's what he wants to be.

I want people to return to the center of their focus, the people that might lose their Medicaid, the people that might lose their Social Security, the veterans right now who are being fired this — for this man who were doing noble work in their continuation of service and helping other veterans. We've got to center those people, elevate our conversation and deepen our fight.

I've learned a hard lesson as a Democrat. We've made a lot of mistakes. We were — we've let a lot of people down. But we've got to learn. And — and I think what people want, as you said, they just want us — they want to know that we hurt and that we're angry, but that we're using that pain and that anger not to consume us or not to turn us into what we're against, but to — to use it as fuel to fight for people.

MELBER: Right. And people have seen versions of this, including protests. I don't want to minimize a lot of what's been going on, but no one's catalyzed this fight who's been in the Democratic Caucus or leadership like this until you this week. So when you hear the talk of — I know — I know you, so I know how you're going to potentially be classy and pivot. But — but there's talk about whether you should be Senate leader for the Democrats. Are you open to that position in the future or running for president again, while we see both the nation and part of the world say, oh, maybe this Cory Booker guy is the new face?

BOOKER: Well, the Senate Democrats have a leader and he's going to remain there. I have an election. New Jersey's got two big elections. We're like Virginia, we've got this November.

MELBER: Yep.

BOOKER: And then I have my re-election in '26. And — and that's why I've got this big state of New Jersey. I want New Jersey to know what my focus is and my priorities are, what they elected me to do. I'm fighting for them. I'll be back in my state doing rallies and town halls this weekend around the issues.

But I want to just challenge you to let you know that maybe here in the Senate, a lot of us have — of my colleagues and I, I've watched them fight. I've watched their grit. I see some of the most amazing human beings. They came out last night for me, whether it was Chris Murphy, who stood with me that entire time, was in the Senate chambers, to Tammy Duckworth, riding in in a wheelchair, lost her legs in combat, to people out there. There's a woman in Iowa named Konfrst, who's been leading — she became a friend of mine when I did run for president. That woman is a fighter. And America may not be paying attention to her, but she is extraordinary. I could...

(CROSSTALK)

MELBER: Well, see...

BOOKER: ... all the states that I know.

MELBER: See, I can say this because — because we've had these conversations over the years, Senator, this is the classy deflection I was talking about.

BOOKER: Right.

MELBER: Because that's great. That's great, substantively. But there, again, my job is to talk to you and everyone else who comes on the show. There are a lot of folks who are saying, OK, Senator Schumer, you had your run, that's great. He's invited on the program. I've had him before. But as you know, we're not going to hide the ball here. There's a lot of conversations about whether over the next few years there should be new leadership.

Before I let you go. You were on this program a while back and we got to the end of the interview. You may not remember, especially after what you've been through this week. And you said, wait, where's my lyric? And I said, well, it's...

(LAUGHTER)

MELBER: I said, it's such a serious day, as it sometimes is, I didn't — we didn't get to one. Well, I brought you one tonight, OK?

BOOKER: OK.

MELBER: Mase, who you remember, "Harlem World," M-A-$-E, on a track featuring the late, great DMX, "If you had 24 hours to live, what would you do? And now I think we know the answer when it comes to you."

BOOKER: Wow. That's deep. Well, I want you to know, before I went out there, there's a great gospel song called "Stand." And it was one of the ones that I use to empower myself. And...

MELBER: What was it called again, "Stand"?

BOOKER: It's called "Stand," S-T-A-N-D.

MELBER: "Stand."

BOOKER: "Stand," yes.

MELBER: And that was your pump up music?

BOOKER: That was one. I lean on a lot of gospel, my brother, to — to stand up and to let me — myself know that my body was not capable of 25 hours, but I knew if I could tap into the spirit, and I think a lot of the spirit that binds us all together, that I can make it through. And so I'm just thankful to all the people that believed and sent me energy and a lot of people who showed up who could.

MELBER: Yep.

BOOKER: And I was able to stand.

MELBER: That is right on point. I love that it's gospel, given some of the themes you shared, both in the speech and tonight. And "Stand," for all those listening or watching around the country, is on Senator Booker's Spotify playlist, apparently. So we always love learning about the playlists.

Senator, you've been very busy. I'm glad you got a seat after all that time standing. Thanks for joining us tonight.

BOOKER: Thank you. Thank you, brother. Thank you, I appreciate you always.

MELBER: Thank you. Senator Cory Booker, the man in the news, the man of the hour, 25 hours, our thanks.

END

data.title: Australia's Service Business Activity Growth Hits Seven-Month High in March
data.description:
Australia's service business activity hit a seven-month high in March, with the seasonally adjusted S&P Global Australia Services PMI Business Activity Index rising to 51.6 from 50.8 in February, according to the results of S&P Global's survey released Thursday.

Growth in new business accelerated at the fastest pace in almost three years due to better economic conditions, marketing efforts, and growing client interest, the report said.

However, export orders declined due to disruptions from cyclone Alfred.

Job creation grew at the fastest pace in nearly two years, the report added.

The Composite Output Index rose to 51.6 in March from 50.6 in February, marking the fastest pace since August 2024 and a sixth straight increase in business activity.

Fuel Prices Fall After Trump Talks Tariffs
data.description:
By Ryan Dezember

Oil, gasoline and diesel prices are dropping following President Trump's unveiling of his tariff plan, reflecting energy traders' concerns that fuel demand will decline as import taxes slow economic activity.

Benchmark U.S. crude futures recently traded below $70 a barrel, down more than 2%, or about $2, from energy markets' afternoon settlement price. Gasoline and diesel futures in New York each dropped by more than 3%.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: U.S. Effective Tariff Rate Highest in 131 YearsMarket Talk
data.description:
1909 ET - President Trump's country-specific reciprocal tariffs turned out to be bigger than expected. The announcements imply the effective tariff rate on all imports will rise to around 26% in 2025 from 2.3% last year, leaving it at a 131-year high, says Neil Shearing, Group Chief Economist at Capital Economics. In very general terms, Canada and Mexico have gotten off lightly, while nations in Asia, particularly China and Vietnam, have been hit hard, he adds. The European Union and Japan sit somewhere in the middle, Shearing said. (james.glynn@wsj.com; @JamesGlynnWSJ)

1856 ET - It was a machine gun spray from President Trump on tariffs so everyone caught a few bullets. Australia will have a 10% tariff on all exports to the U.S., so when compared to China and Europe it could have been worse, says Peter Jolly, the former head of global economic research at National Australia Bank. The key theme about tariffs is that currency markets will move to offset their impact in both countries, but an upward drift in goods prices still seems unavoidable, Jolly says. The big worry is whether businesses hold back investment and employment decisions due to uncertainty around global trade policy, he adds. (james.glynn@wsj.com; @JamesGlynnWSJ)

1856 ET - U.S. equity futures have fallen more than 2.3% following President Trump's far more hawkish than expected tariff announcements. The tariff rates far exceed baseline expectations, and if they aren't negotiated down promptly, expectations for a recession in the U.S. will rise dramatically, says Tony Sycamore, market analyst at IG. Markets are very likely to see Goldman Sachs revise higher their 12-month estimate of a recession in the U.S. from 35% to 50%, he adds. U.S. equity markets aren't priced for a recession, and in the event of a recession in the U.S., stock markets could easily fall another 10%-15% below their mid-March lows, he adds. (james.glynn@wsj.com; @JamesGlynnWSJ)

1850 ET - Economists at Piper Sandler write that the newly announced tariffs are more dramatic and darker for the U.S. economic outlook than they had expected. Piper Sandler's Nancy Lazar and Jake Oubina expects that tariffs will raise U.S. prices by 2.6 percentage points, with a significant corresponding negative hit to economic growth. Piper Sandler cuts its 2Q GDP forecast to minus 1%, from flat. "Given where the big tariff hikes are concentrated--China, Vietnam, Taiwan, etc.--the risks to consumer inflation are much higher than initially thought," the firm writes. (matt.grossman@wsj.com, @mattgrossman)(matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

1842 ET - President Trump's tariff announcement is "worse than the worst case scenario" the street was fearing, say Wedbush analysts Daniel Ives and Scott Devitt. While many details are to be worked out, investors will focus on specifics in the next 24 hours. The jaw-dropper was the 34% reciprocal tariff on China, followed by 32% on Taiwan, along with EU at 20%, they say in a note. Tech stocks will be under pressure due to concerns about demand destruction, supply chain disruptions, and particularly the China-Taiwan aspect of the tariffs, they say. (venkat.pr@wsj.com)

1812 ET - The Trump administration's decision to largely exempt Canada from its tariff plan could set a path for "real negotiation" toward a new economic-and-security pact between the U.S. and its northern neighbor, says the head of Canada's largest business-lobby group. Candace Laing, president of the Canadian Chamber of Commerce, says the world "is waking up today to a reality that Canada has been living with for months." She says the chain reaction of new U.S. tariffs will have severe repercussions on Americans, Canadians and the global economy. President Trump has repeatedly promised hefty 25% on most nonenergy Canadian imports. In a surprise, his tariff order brings no additional new duties on Canadian imports outside of what was previously announced ahead of Wednesday. (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1748 ET - Steep new tariffs on Southeast Asian countries will sting manufacturers that moved operations there in order to avoid previous rounds of U.S. tariffs against China. U.S. imports from Thailand, for instance, grew by 12.5% last year to $63.3 billion, and imports from Vietnam grew by 19% year over year to hit $136.6 billion. According to President Trump's tariff chart, the U.S. will now charge a 46% tariff against Vietnam and a 36% tariff against Thailand. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

1740 ET - The USD's reaction to the announcement of U.S. reciprocal tariffs has been mixed so far, and is expected to develop further through coming hours as the trading day in Asia gets underway and the policy changes are better understood, currency traders say. The U.S. dollar index is current weaker and trading at around 103.71. The conversation about tariffs is not at an end, says Kristina Clifton, currency strategist at CBA. Countries may negotiate to have tariffs delayed or lowered, she says. If the tariffs are seen to pose large disruptions to U.S. business or consumers, then Trump may lower the tariffs or simply not collect them, Clifton adds. (james.glynn@wsj.com; @JamesGlynnWSJ)

1716 ET - The Mexican peso reverses course and strengthens against the U.S. dollar as USMCA partners Mexico and Canada are absent from the list of countries facing additional U.S. reciprocal trade tariffs. "This is good news, above all because of the exemptions made for goods exported to the U.S. under the USMCA," Banco Base's head of analysis Gabriela Siller says in a post on X. Mexico is included in global U.S. tariffs on steel and aluminum, and President Trump said a 25% tariff on all foreign-made automobiles will take effect at midnight. The peso trades at 20.16 to the U.S. dollar versus 20.35 Tuesday. The Mexican stock market's benchmark IPC index closes up 0.9%. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1714 ET - Fed governor Adriana Kugler wants to see upside risks to inflation abate before the Fed returns to cutting interest rates. Speaking at Princeton University, she notes that several measures of expected inflation have increased, and observes that while housing-services inflation has been improving, there have been setbacks in core-goods inflation and market-based services inflation. "I will support maintaining the current policy rate for as long as these upside risks to inflation continue, while economic activity and employment remain stable," Kugler say. "Going forward, I will carefully assess incoming data, the evolving outlook, and changes in the balance of risks." (matt.grossman@wsj.com, @mattgrossman)

1703 ET - Treasury yields give away gains and fall in late trade following Trump's tariff announcements. Stock futures and dollar indexes also decline. The President Trump says the U.S. will impose a 10% across-the-board tariff on all imports, plus reciprocal levies on several countries. Upcoming data will likely be watched for signs of how trade wars will impact consumer prices and economic growth. Some analysts expect the clarification about tariffs to bringcalm to Wall Street and rekindle animal spirits. The 10-year yield is at 4.145% and the two-year at 3.860%. The WSJ Dollar Index falls 0.3% and the ICE US Dollar gauge is down 0.8%. S&P 500 futures fall 1.4%. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1655 ET - Markets are discounting the possibility of near-term Fed rate cuts after President Trump's tariff announcement. The implied odds of a quarter-point cut in May have decreased to 13%, from 19% before the announcement. The implied odds that the Fed won't cut rates at all through June has climbed to 31%, from 24% pre-announcement. Yet traders are still showing a strong conviction that the Fed will return to cutting rates before the end of 2025, with expectations centered on the possibilities of one, two or three cuts this year. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

data.title: Market Talk Roundup: Latest on U.S. Politics
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering U.S. politics. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1909 ET - President Trump's country-specific reciprocal tariffs turned out to be bigger than expected. The announcements imply the effective tariff rate on all imports will rise to around 26% in 2025 from 2.3% last year, leaving it at a 131-year high, says Neil Shearing, Group Chief Economist at Capital Economics. In very general terms, Canada and Mexico have gotten off lightly, while nations in Asia, particularly China and Vietnam, have been hit hard, he adds. The European Union and Japan sit somewhere in the middle, Shearing said. (james.glynn@wsj.com; @JamesGlynnWSJ)

1901 ET - U.S. President Trump's tariff announcement has "caused some impressive gyrations in markets," IG's Chris Beauchamp says in an email. "The full range of tariffs deals a remarkable blow to the global trading system, as the U.S. enacts steep charges on its main trading partners," the chief market analyst say. "This scenario is far worse than had been anticipated. After a brief relief rally (overnight), the outlook for equity markets seems grim," Beauchamp says. This news has sent equity futures sharply lower, Beauchamp adds. eMini NASDAQ 100 futures last down 4.3%; eMini S&P 500 futures last 3.5% lower; eMini Dow futures last 2.4% down. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

1859 ET - Former USTR Carla Hills calls President Trump's new tariff regime "crazy policy" and "tremendously harmful." Hills, who was the chief trade official under President George H.W. Bush, says the U.S. and global economy is now in a period of elevated uncertainty. "This incredulous trade action will have a very adverse effect here in the U.S.," Hills told the Canadian Broadcasting Corp. She adds that she fears for the future of North American trade. She helped finalize the original NAFTA trade pact, which was later renegotiated during Trump's first term and renamed USMCA. (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1857 ET - Wayfair and other furniture companies shifted sourcing to a variety of Asian countries after President Trump's first term posed steep levies on China, the company's Chief Executive Niraj Shah said on an earnings call in February. "There's sources inside Asia, so places like Cambodia, Indonesia, Thailand, Philippines and Vietnam for sure, that have grown as places where folks have factories and where our goods are coming from," Shah told analysts at the time. The stock's fall, down 15% in after-hours trading, suggests its efforts to shift its supply chain could now be in vain as Trump's new tariffs promise some of the biggest levies on several of its now-key suppliers, including Indonesia and Vietnam, with levies of 32% and 46%, respectively.(kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1856 ET - U.S. equity futures have fallen more than 2.3% following President Trump's far more hawkish than expected tariff announcements. The tariff rates far exceed baseline expectations, and if they aren't negotiated down promptly, expectations for a recession in the U.S. will rise dramatically, says Tony Sycamore, market analyst at IG. Markets are very likely to see Goldman Sachs revise higher their 12-month estimate of a recession in the U.S. from 35% to 50%, he adds. U.S. equity markets aren't priced for a recession, and in the event of a recession in the U.S., stock markets could easily fall another 10%-15% below their mid-March lows, he adds. (james.glynn@wsj.com; @JamesGlynnWSJ)

1850 ET - Economists at Piper Sandler write that the newly announced tariffs are more dramatic and darker for the U.S. economic outlook than they had expected. Piper Sandler's Nancy Lazar and Jake Oubina expects that tariffs will raise U.S. prices by 2.6 percentage points, with a significant corresponding negative hit to economic growth. Piper Sandler cuts its 2Q GDP forecast to minus 1%, from flat. "Given where the big tariff hikes are concentrated--China, Vietnam, Taiwan, etc.--the risks to consumer inflation are much higher than initially thought," the firm writes. (matt.grossman@wsj.com, @mattgrossman)(matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

1842 ET - President Trump's tariff announcement is "worse than the worst case scenario" the street was fearing, say Wedbush analysts Daniel Ives and Scott Devitt. While many details are to be worked out, investors will focus on specifics in the next 24 hours. The jaw-dropper was the 34% reciprocal tariff on China, followed by 32% on Taiwan, along with EU at 20%, they say in a note. Tech stocks will be under pressure due to concerns about demand destruction, supply chain disruptions, and particularly the China-Taiwan aspect of the tariffs, they say. (venkat.pr@wsj.com)

1838 ET - RH has been further diversifying its manufacturing network since the company realized China was going to be a long term problem, CEO Gary Friedman says on a call with analysts. "Now it looks like moving out of China didn't make a difference," the executive says. The game has changed and it doesn't matter where you go, except for America and the U.S., where the company has been expanding its capabilities, he says. "It's going to be messy, but it's going to be messy for everyone," Friedman says, while acknowledging that he doesn't like to be caught off guard. Overall, RH can navigate the current environment "pretty well" over the short term, the executive adds. RH shares fall 25% to $186 in post-market trading. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1831 ET - VF, which owns brands including North Face, Vans and Timberland, has been waging a turnaround. President Trump's sweeping new tariff plan that calls for the U.S. to impose a 10% tariff on all imports and much higher rates for some nations, will likely make its revitalization efforts more difficult. During its fiscal 2024, VF's products were sourced from independent manufacturers primarily located in Asia, the company said in its most recent annual filing with the Securities and Exchange Commission. A slew of events, including 34% duties on China, could hurt the company's ability to source or deliver products, as well as its profitability, it says. Shares fall 5.2% in late trading. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1812 ET - The Trump administration's decision to largely exempt Canada from its tariff plan could set a path for "real negotiation" toward a new economic-and-security pact between the U.S. and its northern neighbor, says the head of Canada's largest business-lobby group. Candace Laing, president of the Canadian Chamber of Commerce, says the world "is waking up today to a reality that Canada has been living with for months." She says the chain reaction of new U.S. tariffs will have severe repercussions on Americans, Canadians and the global economy. President Trump has repeatedly promised hefty 25% on most nonenergy Canadian imports. In a surprise, his tariff order brings no additional new duties on Canadian imports outside of what was previously announced ahead of Wednesday. (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1740 ET - The USD's reaction to the announcement of U.S. reciprocal tariffs has been mixed so far, and is expected to develop further through coming hours as the trading day in Asia gets underway and the policy changes are better understood, currency traders say. The U.S. dollar index is current weaker and trading at around 103.71. The conversation about tariffs is not at an end, says Kristina Clifton, currency strategist at CBA. Countries may negotiate to have tariffs delayed or lowered, she says. If the tariffs are seen to pose large disruptions to U.S. business or consumers, then Trump may lower the tariffs or simply not collect them, Clifton adds. (james.glynn@wsj.com; @JamesGlynnWSJ)

1735 ET - Lululemon Athletica thought it was safe from President Trump's tariffs. That wasn't the case. The Vancouver-based company doesn't make its yoga pants and running shorts in Canada, which previously gave it a competitive advantage over other Canadian companies that do manufacture domestically, such as Canada Goose. The company, however, produces nearly 90% of its athletic gear in Vietnam, Cambodia, Sri Lanka, Indonesia and Bangladesh. Shares fall 12% in late trading after President Trump announced reciprocal tariffs of 46% on Vietnam, 49% on Cambodia, 44% on Sri Lanka, 32% on Indonesia and 37% on Bangladesh. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

data.title: Call for President's Rule for Singapore Before GE2025: “Global Citizens' Manifesto” Exposes Singapore’s Political Reality
data.description:
The book "Global Citizens' Manifesto: Happy New Democracy" by Singaporean author EShan V challenges Singapore's political system, proposing AI-enhanced governance to replace pseudo-democracy. It advocates for President's Rule before the 2025 elections and promotes direct dynamic democracy over regency republics.

SINGAPORE, April 02, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — A Singaporean in his sixties has joined forces with artificial intelligence (AI) to craft a bold democratic model that challenges Singapore's political structure. His work, "Global Citizens' Manifesto: Happy New Democracy," demonstrates how partnerships with AI can create practical alternatives to existing governance systems.

Singapore, long criticized for tight political controls, restricted media freedom, and notorious for gaslighting people through mass media, now faces a compelling alternative through this publication. "Global Citizens' Manifesto" presents the first comprehensive blueprint for democratic reform, tailored explicitly to small nations yet applicable worldwide, with Singapore positioned as an ideal testing ground.

"Our current democratic systems have fundamental flaws that prevent true representation. AI offers us tools to correct these imbalances and return political power to citizens in meaningful ways," states EShan, creator of "Global Citizens' Manifesto."

The manifesto calls for President's Rule in Singapore before the GE2025 elections while the UN conducts a “Democracy Compliance Check,” arguing that the current system represents a pseudo-democracy that requires fundamental restructuring. It proposes a direct dynamic democracy model to replace what EShan terms the “Regency Republic,” which has dominated democracies worldwide.

The book introduces industry/profession-based constituency structures for democratic nations, ensuring that governance reflects diverse expertise across economic sectors. This structure replaces what EShan calls an “Elected Oligarchy,” where electoral mechanisms create an illusion of representation while concentrating power among elites.

The publication is critical for Singapore's citizens, particularly regarding financial autonomy. Recent changes to Singapore's Central Provident Fund have greatly concerned the author, as the government has introduced a retirement account (RA) system that effectively restricts citizens from accessing over $200,000 of their life savings. With Provident Fund contributions being the highest in the world, EShan now doubts the safety of these funds and whether the government has devised a scheme to manage withdrawals using fresh inflows, all while maintaining its confidence to win elections indefinitely.

"As Abraham Lincoln famously noted, you cannot fool all the people all the time," EShan says. "The absence of meaningful opposition questioning these fundamental changes to citizens' financial freedoms reveals the staged nature of Singapore's multi-party system."

This manifesto pushes beyond theoretical critique by outlining a decentralized governance system supported by AI capabilities. The proposed framework moves past the limitations of representative democracy toward direct citizen participation in policy formation and implementation.

Released amid growing global conversations about democracy's resilience and technology's applications in governance, this book showcases how human insight, combined with machine intelligence, can tackle complex political challenges that have frustrated reformers for generations.

The author is currently engaged in translating his work into all languages worldwide. Publishers interested in collaborating with the author can send publishing proposals directly via email, mentioning their language of choice.

"Global Citizens' Manifesto: Happy New Democracy" is available on Kindle for readers interested in exploring its arguments and proposals. The timing coincides with increasing demands for democratic renewal, offering concrete steps toward governance systems built on technological innovation and substantive citizen involvement.

The companion volume, "Global Citizens' Manifesto. Solution to World Water Crisis: The Culprit that Causes Water Crisis, Identified," explores a critical challenge facing humanity. It examines how the outdated siphon toilet system contributes to excessive water waste and presents innovative “Virtual Siphon Toilet” for conservation and sustainable sanitation solutions.

data.title: Canada, Mexico Get Short-Term Advantage From Trump's Tariff MovesMarket Talk
data.description: Desjardins Group economist Royce Mendes says President Trump's new tariff policy, as it applies to Canada, reinforces the firm's view that the Bank of Canada will remain on hold on April 16, or its next rate-policy announcement. Canada and Mexico will face no new duties, other than what was previously announced prior to Wednesday. Mendes says this gives Canada and Mexico "some short-term advantage" over other countries. As a result there is some scope for the two countries to take market share from other U.S. trading partners. Mendes warns that while there remain unresolved trade irritants between the U.S. and Canada, traders might interpret the Liberation Day moves as a starting point for negotiations on a new North American trade pact. (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com, @paulvieira)

data.title: AsiaPhos Former Unit Ordered to Pay 3 Million Yuan in Legal Dispute
data.description:
AsiaPhos said a Chinese court has ordered its former subsidiary, Sichuan Mianzhu Norwest Phosphate Chemical, to pay 3 million yuan in compensation to Huili Jiahong Chemical, along with a court handling fee of 30,840 yuan, according to a Wednesday filing on the Singapore Exchange.

The ruling by the Deyang Intermediate People's Court is final and Sichuan Mianzhu Norwest Phosphate intends to proceed with payment while consulting legal advisers on a potential retrial application.

AsiaPhos said it had already recognized an impairment loss of 3 million yuan in fiscal 2024.
